questions.solutionText: Solution V = π Pr 4 8 η l = 8 cm 3 s - 1 For composite tube V 1 = P π r 4 8 η ( l + l 2 ) = 2 3 π Pr 4 8 η l = 2 3 × 8 = 1 6 3 cm 3 s - 1 ∴ l 1 = l = 2 l 2 or l 2 = l 2
questions.solution: Solution V = π Pr 4 8 η l = 8 cm 3 s - 1 For composite tube V 1 = P π r 4 8 η ( l + l 2 ) = 2 3 π Pr 4 8 η l = 2 3 × 8 = 1 6 3 cm 3 s - 1 ∴ l 1 = l = 2 l 2 or l 2 = l 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
V=πPr48ηl=8 cm3 s-1
For composite tube
V1=Pπr48η(l+l2)=23πPr48ηl=23×8=163 cm3 s-1∴l1=l=2l2orl2=l2

questions.questionHtml: An inverted bell, lying at the bottom of lake 47.6 m deep, has 50 cm3 of air trapped in it. The bell is brought to the surface of the lake, then the volume of the trapped air will become [atmospheric pressure = 70 cm of Hg and density of Hg=13.6 g cm-3]
questions.solutionText: Solution According to Boyle's law, pressure and volume are inversely proportional to each other i.e. p ∝ 1 v ⇒ P 1 V 1 = P 2 V 2 ⇒ ( P 0 + h ρ w g ) V 1 = P 0 V 2 ⇒ V 2 = ( 1 + h ρ w g P 0 ) V 1 V 2 = ( 1 + 47.6 * 1 * 1000 * 10 70 * 10 − 2 * 13.6 * 1000 * 10 ) [ A s P 2 = P 0 = 70 c m o f H g = 70 * 10 − 2 * 13.6 * 1000 * 10 ] ⇒ V 2 = 1 + 5 5 0 cm 3 = 3 0 0 cm 3
questions.solution: Solution According to Boyle's law, pressure and volume are inversely proportional to each other i.e. p ∝ 1 v ⇒ P 1 V 1 = P 2 V 2 ⇒ ( P 0 + h ρ w g ) V 1 = P 0 V 2 ⇒ V 2 = ( 1 + h ρ w g P 0 ) V 1 V 2 = ( 1 + 47.6 * 1 * 1000 * 10 70 * 10 − 2 * 13.6 * 1000 * 10 ) [ A s P 2 = P 0 = 70 c m o f H g = 70 * 10 − 2 * 13.6 * 1000 * 10 ] ⇒ V 2 = 1 + 5 5 0 cm 3 = 3 0 0 cm 3
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
According to Boyle's law, pressure and volume are inversely proportional to each other i.e. p∝1v

⇒P1V1=P2V2
⇒(P0+hρwg)V1=P0V2
⇒V2=(1+hρwgP0)V1
V2=(1+47.6*1*1000*1070*10−2*13.6*1000*10)[As P2=P0=70 cm of Hg=70*10−2*13.6*1000*10]⇒V2=1+550cm3=300cm3

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
y=5sin⁡(4t+π3) m

ω=4 rad/s, A=5 m, m=2 g =2×10−3 kg

T=2πω=π2 s, so T4=π8 s

At t=π8:

y=5sin⁡(π2+π3)=5cos⁡π3=52 m

KE=12mω2(A2−y2)=12×2×10−3×16×(25−254)

=12×2×10−3×16×754

=10−3×16×754=10−3×300=0.3 J

Answer: (4) 0.3 J

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A spherical ball of mass m is kept at the highest point in the space between two fixed, concentric spheres A and B (see fig.). The smaller sphere A has a radius R and the space between the two spheres has a width d. The ball has a diameter very slightly less than d. All surfaces are frictionless. The ball is given a gentle push (towards the right in the figure). The angle made by the radius vector of the ball with the upward vertical is denoted by θ. What is the total normal reaction force exerted by the spheres on the ball in terms of angle θ ?

questions.solutionText: Solution h = R + d 2 1 - cos θ Velocity of ball at angle θ is ν 2 = 2 gh = 2 R + d 2 1 - cos θ g ...... (i) Let N be the normal reaction (away from centre) at angle θ. Then, mg cos θ - N = m ν 2 R + d 2 Substituting value of ν 2 from equation (i), we get mg cos θ - N = 2 mg 1 - cos θ N = mg 3 cos θ - 2
questions.solution: Solution h = R + d 2 1 - cos θ Velocity of ball at angle θ is ν 2 = 2 gh = 2 R + d 2 1 - cos θ g ...... (i) Let N be the normal reaction (away from centre) at angle θ. Then, mg cos θ - N = m ν 2 R + d 2 Substituting value of ν 2 from equation (i), we get mg cos θ - N = 2 mg 1 - cos θ N = mg 3 cos θ - 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
h=R+d21-cos θ



Velocity of ball at angle θ is
ν2=2gh=2R+d21-cos θg ...... (i)
Let N be the normal reaction (away from centre) at angle θ. Then, mg cos θ-N=mν2R+d2
Substituting value of ν2 from equation (i), we get
mg cos θ-N=2 mg1-cos θ
N=mg3 cos θ-2

questions.solutionText: Solution For real inverted image formed by concave mirror. v = − v e , u = − v e , f = − v e ⇒ u f & v f are positive So graph show be in 1st qudarant and 1 v + 1 u = 1 f ⇒ f v + f u = 1 This graph can also be realized by Newtons formula x 1 x 2 = f 2 where x 1 , and x 2 are distance from focus so graph will be rectangular hyperbola ⇒ (1) is right answer.
questions.solution: Solution For real inverted image formed by concave mirror. v = − v e , u = − v e , f = − v e ⇒ u f & v f are positive So graph show be in 1st qudarant and 1 v + 1 u = 1 f ⇒ f v + f u = 1 This graph can also be realized by Newtons formula x 1 x 2 = f 2 where x 1 , and x 2 are distance from focus so graph will be rectangular hyperbola ⇒ (1) is right answer.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For real inverted image formed by concave mirror.
v=−ve, u=−ve, f=−ve
⇒ uf & vf are positive
So graph show be in 1st qudarant
and
1v+1u=1f
⇒ fv+fu=1
This graph can also be realized by Newtons formula x1x2=f2 where x1, and x2 are distance from focus so graph will be rectangular hyperbola
⇒ (1) is right answer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A 5000 kg rocket is set for vertical firing. The exhaust speed is 800 m s - 1 . To give an upward acceleration of 20 m s - 2 , the amount of gas ejected per second to supply the needed thrust is g = 10 m s - 2
questions.questionText: A 5000 kg rocket is set for vertical firing. The exhaust speed is 800 m s - 1 . To give an upward acceleration of 20 m s - 2 , the amount of gas ejected per second to supply the needed thrust is g = 10 m s - 2
questions.question: A 5000 kg rocket is set for vertical firing. The exhaust speed is 800 m s - 1 . To give an upward acceleration of 20 m s - 2 , the amount of gas ejected per second to supply the needed thrust is g = 10 m s - 2
questions.prompt: A 5000 kg rocket is set for vertical firing. The exhaust speed is 800 m s - 1 . To give an upward acceleration of 20 m s - 2 , the amount of gas ejected per second to supply the needed thrust is g = 10 m s - 2
questions.html: A 5000 kg rocket is set for vertical firing. The exhaust speed is 800 m s-1. To give an upward acceleration of 20 m s-2, the amount of gas ejected per second to supply the needed thrust is g=10 m s-2
questions.questionHtml: A 5000 kg rocket is set for vertical firing. The exhaust speed is 800 m s-1. To give an upward acceleration of 20 m s-2, the amount of gas ejected per second to supply the needed thrust is g=10 m s-2
questions.solutionText: Solution Given that, Mass of rocket m = 5000 Kg Acceleration a = 20 m / s 2 Speed v = 800 m / s g = 10 m / s 2 Now, thrust force on the rocket F t = v r ( − dm dt ) Net force on the rocket F net = F t − W m a = v r ( − d m d t ) − m g ( − d m d t ) = m ( g + a ) v r Rate of gas ejected per second ( − dm dt ) = 5000 ( 10 + 20 ) 800 − dm dt = 187.5 kg / s Hence, the amount of gas ejected per second is 187.5 kg / s
questions.solution: Solution Given that, Mass of rocket m = 5000 Kg Acceleration a = 20 m / s 2 Speed v = 800 m / s g = 10 m / s 2 Now, thrust force on the rocket F t = v r ( − dm dt ) Net force on the rocket F net = F t − W m a = v r ( − d m d t ) − m g ( − d m d t ) = m ( g + a ) v r Rate of gas ejected per second ( − dm dt ) = 5000 ( 10 + 20 ) 800 − dm dt = 187.5 kg / s Hence, the amount of gas ejected per second is 187.5 kg / s
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given that,
Mass of rocket m=5000Kg
Acceleration a=20 m/s2
Speed v =800 m/s
g=10 m/s2
Now, thrust force on the rocket
Ft=vr(−dmdt)
Net force on the rocket
Fnet =Ft−Wma=vr(−dmdt)−mg(−dmdt)=m(g+a)vr
Rate of gas ejected per second
(−dmdt)=5000(10+20)800−dmdt=187.5 kg/s
Hence, the amount of gas ejected per second is 187.5 kg/s

questions.solutionText: Solution The following figure represents a free body diagram of the bob. The components of pseudo force and tension in the horizontal and vertical directions are also shown in the figure. On writing the equilibrium equations for horizontal and vertical directions, T sin α = m a cos θ … ( 1 ) T cos α = m a sin θ + m g … ( 2 ) From equations ( 1 ) and ( 2 ) , we obtain the expression, tan α = a cos θ a sin θ + g … ( 3 ) In the question, it is clearly given that a = 10 m s - 2 and θ = 30 ° . On substituting these values in equation ( 3 ) , we get, tan α = 1 3 or α = 30 ° . ^
questions.solution: Solution The following figure represents a free body diagram of the bob. The components of pseudo force and tension in the horizontal and vertical directions are also shown in the figure. On writing the equilibrium equations for horizontal and vertical directions, T sin α = m a cos θ … ( 1 ) T cos α = m a sin θ + m g … ( 2 ) From equations ( 1 ) and ( 2 ) , we obtain the expression, tan α = a cos θ a sin θ + g … ( 3 ) In the question, it is clearly given that a = 10 m s - 2 and θ = 30 ° . On substituting these values in equation ( 3 ) , we get, tan α = 1 3 or α = 30 ° . ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

The following figure represents a free body diagram of the bob. The components of pseudo force and tension in the horizontal and vertical directions are also shown in the figure.

On writing the equilibrium equations for horizontal and vertical directions,

Tsinα=macosθ …(1) Tcosα=masinθ+mg …(2)

From equations (1) and (2), we obtain the expression,

tanα=acosθasinθ+g …(3)

In the question, it is clearly given thata=10 m s-2 and θ=30°.

On substituting these values in equation (3), we get,

tanα=13
or α=30°.


^

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Assuming all the surfaces to be smooth, find the time period of oscillation of the ball released from the top of the wedge.
questions.questionText: Assuming all the surfaces to be smooth, find the time period of oscillation of the ball released from the top of the wedge.
questions.question: Assuming all the surfaces to be smooth, find the time period of oscillation of the ball released from the top of the wedge.
questions.prompt: Assuming all the surfaces to be smooth, find the time period of oscillation of the ball released from the top of the wedge.
questions.html: Assuming all the surfaces to be smooth, find the time period of oscillation of the ball released from the top of the wedge.
questions.questionHtml: Assuming all the surfaces to be smooth, find the time period of oscillation of the ball released from the top of the wedge.
questions.solutionText: Solution Since all surfaces are frictionless, and both wedges are of similar, so time of ascent and time of descent will be same for both. Hence, time period of motion is given by sum of time of ascent and time of descent of both. Time period, T = t a + t d Ist wedge + t a + t d 2 nd wedge ⇒ T = 4 × t d Suppose, time of ascent = time of descent = t d . Using second equation of motion, s = u t + 1 2 a t 2 . Here, Acceleration along inclined plane, a = g sin 30 ° = g 2 Displacement along the inclined plane, s = 40 sin 30 ° = 80 cm = 0 . 8 m 0 . 8 = 1 2 × 10 2 × t 2 ⇒ t = 0 . 8 × 2 × 2 10 = 2 2 5 s Time period = 4 t = 8 2 5 s . `
questions.solution: Solution Since all surfaces are frictionless, and both wedges are of similar, so time of ascent and time of descent will be same for both. Hence, time period of motion is given by sum of time of ascent and time of descent of both. Time period, T = t a + t d Ist wedge + t a + t d 2 nd wedge ⇒ T = 4 × t d Suppose, time of ascent = time of descent = t d . Using second equation of motion, s = u t + 1 2 a t 2 . Here, Acceleration along inclined plane, a = g sin 30 ° = g 2 Displacement along the inclined plane, s = 40 sin 30 ° = 80 cm = 0 . 8 m 0 . 8 = 1 2 × 10 2 × t 2 ⇒ t = 0 . 8 × 2 × 2 10 = 2 2 5 s Time period = 4 t = 8 2 5 s . `
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Since all surfaces are frictionless, and both wedges are of similar, so time of ascent and time of descent will be same for both.
Hence, time period of motion is given by sum of time of ascent and time of descent of both.
Time period,
T=ta+tdIst wedge+ta+td2nd wedge

⇒T=4×td

Suppose,
time of ascent=time of descent=td.

Using second equation of motion,

s=ut+12at2.

Here,

Acceleration along inclined plane,
a=gsin30°=g2

Displacement along the inclined plane,
s=40sin30°=80 cm=0.8 m

0.8=12×102×t2

⇒t=0.8×2×210=225 s

Time period =4t=825 s.


`

questions.text: A 100 W sodium lamp radiates energy uniformly in all directions. The lamp is located at the centre of a large sphere that absorbs all the sodium light which is incident on it. The wavelength of the sodium light is 589 nm . At what rate are the photons from the lamp associated with the sodium light?

questions.solutionText: Solution Given, power of lamp, P = 100 W The wavelength of the sodium light, λ = 5 8 9 nm = 589 × 10 - 9 m Planck constant h = 6 . 63 × 10 - 34 J s Let n photons are delivered per second. ∴ n = Power Energy of each photon = P 12431 5890 × 1 . 6 × 10 - 19 J = 1 0 0 3 . 3 8 × 1 0 - 1 9 = 3 × 1 0 2 0 photon s - 1 = 3 × 10 20 photon s - 1 are delivered. ^

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given, power of lamp, P=100 W
The wavelength of the sodium light, λ=589 nm = 589×10-9 m
Planck constant h=6.63×10-34 J s
Let n photons are delivered per second.
∴ n=PowerEnergy of each photon=P124315890×1.6×10-19 J
=1003.38×10-19=3×1020 photon s-1
=3×1020 photon s-1 are delivered.
^

questions.questionHtml: On which of the following factors internal resistance of a cell depends:
questions.solutionText: Solution Factors affecting internal resistance of a cell: (i) Larger the separation between the electrodes of the cell, more the length of the electrolyte through which current has to flow. (ii) Greater the conductivity of electrolyte, lesser is the resistance. (iii) The internal resistance is inversely proportional to the common area of electrodes dipped in electrolyte. :
questions.solution: Solution Factors affecting internal resistance of a cell: (i) Larger the separation between the electrodes of the cell, more the length of the electrolyte through which current has to flow. (ii) Greater the conductivity of electrolyte, lesser is the resistance. (iii) The internal resistance is inversely proportional to the common area of electrodes dipped in electrolyte. :
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Factors affecting internal resistance of a cell:

(i) Larger the separation between the electrodes of the cell, more the length of the electrolyte through which current has to flow.

(ii) Greater the conductivity of electrolyte, lesser is the resistance.

(iii) The internal resistance is inversely proportional to the common area of electrodes dipped in electrolyte.


:

questions.solutionText: Solution Speed of transverse wave on a string: v = T μ μ = m L = 2.10 12.0 = 0.175 kg/m v = 343 m/s T = μ v 2 = 0.175 × ( 343 ) 2 = 0.175 × 117649 = 20588.6 N T ≈ 2.06 × 10 4 N Answer: (1) 2.06 × 10 4 N
questions.solution: Solution Speed of transverse wave on a string: v = T μ μ = m L = 2.10 12.0 = 0.175 kg/m v = 343 m/s T = μ v 2 = 0.175 × ( 343 ) 2 = 0.175 × 117649 = 20588.6 N T ≈ 2.06 × 10 4 N Answer: (1) 2.06 × 10 4 N
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Speed of transverse wave on a string: v=Tμ

μ=mL=2.1012.0=0.175 kg/m

v=343 m/s

T=μv2=0.175×(343)2=0.175×117649=20588.6 N

T≈2.06×104 N

questions.html:
One mole of ideal gas goes through the following transformations:

Step I: Isochoric cooling to 13 of the initial temperature.
Step II: Adiabatic compression to its initial pressure.
Step III: Isobaric expansion back to the initial state.
If the initial temperature and pressure are 600 K and 3.00 atm, and if the ideal gas is monoatomic CV=32 R, what is the work for the third step (going from state 3 to state 1)?
questions.questionHtml:
One mole of ideal gas goes through the following transformations:

Step I: Isochoric cooling to 13 of the initial temperature.
Step II: Adiabatic compression to its initial pressure.
Step III: Isobaric expansion back to the initial state.
If the initial temperature and pressure are 600 K and 3.00 atm, and if the ideal gas is monoatomic CV=32 R, what is the work for the third step (going from state 3 to state 1)?
questions.solutionText: Solution At state 2 : p 2 = 1 bar , T 2 = 200 K At state 3 : p = 3 bar Applying the adiabatic conditions between 2 and 3 : PT γ 1 - γ = constant γ 1 - γ = 5 / 3 1 - 5 / 3 = - 5 2 ⇒ 1.0 2 0 0 - 5 2 = 3 T 3 - 5 2 ⇒ T 3 = 310 K ⇒ W 3 1 = - p Δ V = - R Δ T = - 0.082 6 0 0 - 3 1 0 = - 0.082 × 2 9 0 = - 23.8 L atm .
questions.solution: Solution At state 2 : p 2 = 1 bar , T 2 = 200 K At state 3 : p = 3 bar Applying the adiabatic conditions between 2 and 3 : PT γ 1 - γ = constant γ 1 - γ = 5 / 3 1 - 5 / 3 = - 5 2 ⇒ 1.0 2 0 0 - 5 2 = 3 T 3 - 5 2 ⇒ T 3 = 310 K ⇒ W 3 1 = - p Δ V = - R Δ T = - 0.082 6 0 0 - 3 1 0 = - 0.082 × 2 9 0 = - 23.8 L atm .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

At state 2:

p2=1 bar, T2=200 K

At state 3:

p=3 bar

Applying the adiabatic conditions between 2 and 3:

PTγ1-γ=constant

γ1-γ=5/31-5/3=-52

⇒1.0200-52=3T3-52 ⇒T3=310 K

⇒ W31=-pΔV=-RΔT

=-0.082600-310

=-0.082×290=-23.8 L atm.

questions.solutionText: Solution Given, OC → = OB → = OA → = r . OC → and OA → are equal in magnitude and inclined to each other at an angle of 90 ° . And their resultant is given by, OC → + OA → = OC → 2 + OA → 2 ⇒ OC → + OA → = r 2 + r 2 = r 2 . Also, its direction is at 45 ° from horizontal or mid of the two vectors as their magnitudes are same. Now resultant of OC → & OA → along with OB → is shown in the below figure, So their resultant is 2 r . It acts mid-way between OC → and OA → , i.e., along O B . Now, both r and 2 r are along the same line and in the same direction as shown in the figure. So, resultant is given by, R = r + 2 r = r 1 + 2 .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Which of the following statements are true regarding Bohr's model of hydrogen atom? ( I ) Orbiting speed of electron decreases as it shifts to discrete orbits away from the nucleus. ( II ) Radii of allowed orbits of electron are proportional to the principal quantum number. ( III ) Frequency with which electrons orbit around the nucleus in discrete orbits is inversely proportional to the cube of principal quantum number. ( IV ) Binding force with which the electron is bound to the nucleus increases as it shifts to outer orbits. Select the correct answer using the codes given below

(I) Orbiting speed of electron decreases as it shifts to discrete orbits away from the nucleus.

(II) Radii of allowed orbits of electron are proportional to the principal quantum number.

(III) Frequency with which electrons orbit around the nucleus in discrete orbits is inversely proportional to the cube of principal quantum number.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below
questions.questionHtml:
Which of the following statements are true regarding Bohr's model of hydrogen atom?

(I) Orbiting speed of electron decreases as it shifts to discrete orbits away from the nucleus.

(II) Radii of allowed orbits of electron are proportional to the principal quantum number.

(III) Frequency with which electrons orbit around the nucleus in discrete orbits is inversely proportional to the cube of principal quantum number.

(IV) Binding force with which the electron is bound to the nucleus increases as it shifts to outer orbits.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below
questions.solutionText: Solution Speed of electron in n th orbit = 2 . 18 × 10 6 × Z n For hydrogen atom, Z = 1 Speed of electron in n th orbit for hydrogen atom = 2 . 18 × 10 6 n m s - 1 So, as the value of n increases, orbiting speed of electron decreases. Hence, I is correct. Radii of an electron in n th orbit = 0 . 529 n 2 Z A ∘ Radii of an electron for hydrogen atom in n th orbit = 0 . 529 n 2 1 A ∘ Radii are directly proportional to n 2 not to n . Hence, II is incorrect. Time taken by an electron to complete one revolution = 1 . 534 × 10 - 10 × n 3 Z 2 s T is proportional to n 3 Z 2 We know, frequency with which electrons orbit in nucleus f = 1 T So, f is inversely proportional to n 3 for hydrogen atom. Hence, III is correct. Binding force with which electrons is bound to nucleus = K Z e 2 r 2 In hydrogen atom, binding force = K e 2 r 2 And, we know, as we shift to outer orbit, r increases. So, binding force decreases. Hence, IV is incorrect.

Hence, I is correct. Radii of an electron in n th orbit = 0 . 529 n 2 Z A ∘ Radii of an electron for hydrogen atom in n th orbit = 0 . 529 n 2 1 A ∘ Radii are directly proportional to n 2 not to n . Hence, II is incorrect. Time taken by an electron to complete one revolution = 1 . 534 × 10 - 10 × n 3 Z 2 s T is proportional to n 3 Z 2 We know, frequency with which electrons orbit in nucleus f = 1 T So, f is inversely proportional to n 3 for hydrogen atom. Hence, III is correct. Binding force with which electrons is bound to nucleus = K Z e 2 r 2 In hydrogen atom, binding force = K e 2 r 2 And, we know, as we shift to outer orbit, r increases. So, binding force decreases. Hence, IV is incorrect.

=0.529n21 A∘

Hence, II is incorrect.

=1.534×10-10×n3Z2 s

We know, frequency with which electrons orbit in nucleus f=1T

So, f is inversely proportional to n3 for hydrogen atom.

questions.questionHtml: An ideal heat engine has an efficiency η. Temperatures of source and sink are each decreased by 100 K. Then, the efficiency of the engine.
questions.solutionText: Solution η = 1 − T 2 T 1 New efficiency: η ′ = 1 − T 2 − 100 T 1 − 100 Compare T 2 − 100 T 1 − 100 with T 2 T 1 : Cross multiply: T 1 ( T 2 − 100 ) vs T 2 ( T 1 − 100 ) T 1 T 2 − 100 T 1 vs T 1 T 2 − 100 T 2 − 100 T 1 < − 100 T 2 (since T 1 > T 2 ) ⇒ T 2 − 100 T 1 − 100 < T 2 T 1 ⇒ η ′ > η Answer: (1) Increases
questions.solution: Solution η = 1 − T 2 T 1 New efficiency: η ′ = 1 − T 2 − 100 T 1 − 100 Compare T 2 − 100 T 1 − 100 with T 2 T 1 : Cross multiply: T 1 ( T 2 − 100 ) vs T 2 ( T 1 − 100 ) T 1 T 2 − 100 T 1 vs T 1 T 2 − 100 T 2 − 100 T 1 < − 100 T 2 (since T 1 > T 2 ) ⇒ T 2 − 100 T 1 − 100 < T 2 T 1 ⇒ η ′ > η Answer: (1) Increases
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
η=1−T2T1

New efficiency: η′=1−T2−100T1−100

Compare T2−100T1−100 with T2T1:

Cross multiply: T1(T2−100) vs T2(T1−100)

T1T2−100T1 vs T1T2−100T2

−100T1<−100T2 (since T1>T2)

⇒T2−100T1−100<T2T1

⇒η′>η

Answer: (1) Increases

questions.text: Assertion: In case of motion of an ideal fluid in a horizontal tube, where the area of cross-section is minimum, pressure is maximum. Reason: Hydrostatic pressure in different ideal liquids at points of different depth can be same.
questions.questionText: Assertion: In case of motion of an ideal fluid in a horizontal tube, where the area of cross-section is minimum, pressure is maximum. Reason: Hydrostatic pressure in different ideal liquids at points of different depth can be same.
questions.question: Assertion: In case of motion of an ideal fluid in a horizontal tube, where the area of cross-section is minimum, pressure is maximum. Reason: Hydrostatic pressure in different ideal liquids at points of different depth can be same.
questions.prompt: Assertion: In case of motion of an ideal fluid in a horizontal tube, where the area of cross-section is minimum, pressure is maximum. Reason: Hydrostatic pressure in different ideal liquids at points of different depth can be same.
questions.html:
Assertion: In case of motion of an ideal fluid in a horizontal tube, where the area of cross-section is minimum, pressure is maximum.
Reason: Hydrostatic pressure in different ideal liquids at points of different depth can be same.
questions.questionHtml:
Assertion: In case of motion of an ideal fluid in a horizontal tube, where the area of cross-section is minimum, pressure is maximum.
Reason: Hydrostatic pressure in different ideal liquids at points of different depth can be same.
questions.solutionText: Solution According to the equation of continuity, speed of the fluid is maximum at the minimum cross-sectional area of a horizontal tube and so will be the kinetic energy. And according to Bernoulli's theorem, the pressure will be minimum, where kinetic energy is maximum. So Assertion is false. The reason is true, as excess pressure due to fluid is ρgh . So, for fluids of different densities, pressure can be the same at different depths.
questions.solution: Solution According to the equation of continuity, speed of the fluid is maximum at the minimum cross-sectional area of a horizontal tube and so will be the kinetic energy. And according to Bernoulli's theorem, the pressure will be minimum, where kinetic energy is maximum. So Assertion is false. The reason is true, as excess pressure due to fluid is ρgh . So, for fluids of different densities, pressure can be the same at different depths.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

According to the equation of continuity, speed of the fluid is maximum at the minimum cross-sectional area of a horizontal tube and so will be the kinetic energy. And according to Bernoulli's theorem, the pressure will be minimum, where kinetic energy is maximum. So Assertion is false.
The reason is true, as excess pressure due to fluid is ρgh. So, for fluids of different densities, pressure can be the same at different depths.

ΔU=f2nRdT
ΔQ=(f2+1)nRdT

Remaining is used in expansion of the gas = 40%

questions.solutionText: Solution Let oxidation number of Mn be x . We know that, Ox . no. of O = − 2 . So, Ox. no. Mn + 4 ( Ox . no. O ) = − 1 x + 4 ( − 2 ) = − 1 or x − 8 = − 1 or x = + 8 − 1 = + 7 The oxidation number of Mn in [ MnO 4 ] − ion is + 7 .
questions.solution: Solution Let oxidation number of Mn be x . We know that, Ox . no. of O = − 2 . So, Ox. no. Mn + 4 ( Ox . no. O ) = − 1 x + 4 ( − 2 ) = − 1 or x − 8 = − 1 or x = + 8 − 1 = + 7 The oxidation number of Mn in [ MnO 4 ] − ion is + 7 .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let oxidation number of Mn be x.
We know that, Ox. no. of O=−2.
So, Ox. no. Mn +4(Ox. no. O)=−1
x+4(−2)=−1
or x−8=−1
or x=+8−1=+7
The oxidation number of Mn in [MnO4]−ion is +7.

questions.questionHtml:

2. α-D-glucose and β-D-glucose are anomers.
3. α-D-glucose and β-D-glucose are enantiomers.
4. Cellulose is a straight chain polysaccharide made of only β-D-glucose units.
5. Starch is a mixture of amylase and amylopectin, both contain unbranched chain of α-D-glucose units.
questions.solutionText: Solution α -D-glucose and β -glucose, differ in the orientation of − H and − OH groups are first carbon atom. Such isomers are called anomers. Starch is a mixture of amylase and amylopectin but amylase is a straight chain polymer while amylopectin is a branched chain polymer of α -D-glucose.
questions.solution: Solution α -D-glucose and β -glucose, differ in the orientation of − H and − OH groups are first carbon atom. Such isomers are called anomers. Starch is a mixture of amylase and amylopectin but amylase is a straight chain polymer while amylopectin is a branched chain polymer of α -D-glucose.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
α-D-glucose and β-glucose, differ in the orientation of −H and −OH groups are first carbon atom. Such isomers are called anomers.
Starch is a mixture of amylase and amylopectin but amylase is a straight chain polymer while amylopectin is a branched chain polymer of α-D-glucose.

( i ) CH 2 = CH - C + H 2 and CH 2 = CH - CH 2 - C + H 2 ( ii ) CH 3 - NH - C + H 2 and C + H 2 - OH ( iii ) CH 3 - O - CH 2 - C + H 2 and CH 3 - O - C + H 2 ( iv ) CH 3 - C + H - CH 2 CH 2 CH 3 and CH 3 CH 2 - C + H - CH 2 CH 3

( i ) CH 2 = CH - C + H 2 and CH 2 = CH - CH 2 - C + H 2 ( ii ) CH 3 - NH - C + H 2 and C + H 2 - OH ( iii ) CH 3 - O - CH 2 - C + H 2 and CH 3 - O - C + H 2 ( iv ) CH 3 - C + H - CH 2 CH 2 CH 3 and CH 3 CH 2 - C + H - CH 2 CH 3
questions.prompt: In which of the following pairs of carbocations, the first carbocation is more stable than the second? ( i ) CH 2 = CH - C + H 2 and CH 2 = CH - CH 2 - C + H 2 ( ii ) CH 3 - NH - C + H 2 and C + H 2 - OH ( iii ) CH 3 - O - CH 2 - C + H 2 and CH 3 - O - C + H 2 ( iv ) CH 3 - C + H - CH 2 CH 2 CH 3 and CH 3 CH 2 - C + H - CH 2 CH 3
questions.html:
In which of the following pairs of carbocations, the first carbocation is more stable than the second?
(i) CH2=CH-C+H2 and CH2=CH-CH2-C+H2
(ii) CH3-NH-C+H2 and C+H2-OH
(iii) CH3-O-CH2-C+H2 and CH3-O-C+H2
(iv) CH3-C+H-CH2CH2CH3 and CH3CH2-C+H-CH2CH3
questions.questionHtml:
In which of the following pairs of carbocations, the first carbocation is more stable than the second?
(i) CH2=CH-C+H2 and CH2=CH-CH2-C+H2
(ii) CH3-NH-C+H2 and C+H2-OH
(iii) CH3-O-CH2-C+H2 and CH3-O-C+H2
(iv) CH3-C+H-CH2CH2CH3 and CH3CH2-C+H-CH2CH3
questions.solutionText: Solution In the structure ( i ) , given the first carbocation is stabilised by the resonance with double bond but the second carbocation stabilised by the only alpha hydrogen of a methylene group. In the structure ( ii ) , given the first carbocation is stabilised by the resonance effect by lone pair of the nitrogen atom and positive inductive effect of methyl group but the second carbocation stabilised by the only resonance effect by lone pair of the oxygen atom. In the structure ( iii ) , given the first carbocation is stabilised by the two alpha hydrogen of methylene group and destabilised by the negative inductive effect of oxygen atom but the second carbocation stabilised by the resonance effect by lone pair of the oxygen atom. Therefore, the second carbocation is more stable. In the structure ( iv ) ), given the first carbocation is stabilised by the five alpha hydrogen but the second carbocation is stabilised by the four alpha hydrogen.
questions.solution: Solution In the structure ( i ) , given the first carbocation is stabilised by the resonance with double bond but the second carbocation stabilised by the only alpha hydrogen of a methylene group. In the structure ( ii ) , given the first carbocation is stabilised by the resonance effect by lone pair of the nitrogen atom and positive inductive effect of methyl group but the second carbocation stabilised by the only resonance effect by lone pair of the oxygen atom. In the structure ( iii ) , given the first carbocation is stabilised by the two alpha hydrogen of methylene group and destabilised by the negative inductive effect of oxygen atom but the second carbocation stabilised by the resonance effect by lone pair of the oxygen atom. Therefore, the second carbocation is more stable. In the structure ( iv ) ), given the first carbocation is stabilised by the five alpha hydrogen but the second carbocation is stabilised by the four alpha hydrogen.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

In the structure (i), given the first carbocation is stabilised by the resonance with double bond but the second carbocation stabilised by the only alpha hydrogen of a methylene group.
In the structure (ii), given the first carbocation is stabilised by the resonance effect by lone pair of the nitrogen atom and positive inductive effect of methyl group but the second carbocation stabilised by the only resonance effect by lone pair of the oxygen atom.
In the structure (iii), given the first carbocation is stabilised by the two alpha hydrogen of methylene group and destabilised by the negative inductive effect of oxygen atom but the second carbocation stabilised by the resonance effect by lone pair of the oxygen atom. Therefore, the second carbocation is more stable.
In the structure (iv) ), given the first carbocation is stabilised by the five alpha hydrogen but the second carbocation is stabilised by the four alpha hydrogen.

questions.solutionText: Solution A. Methemaglobinemia (Blue baby syndrome) → caused by excess nitrates in drinking water → IV B. Kidney damage → caused by excess lead in drinking water → II C. Bones and teeth damage → caused by excess fluoride in drinking water → V D. Growth of fish is stopped → caused by very low dissolved oxygen (1 ppm) in water → I Answer: (1) A-IV, B-II, C-V, D-I
questions.solution: Solution A. Methemaglobinemia (Blue baby syndrome) → caused by excess nitrates in drinking water → IV B. Kidney damage → caused by excess lead in drinking water → II C. Bones and teeth damage → caused by excess fluoride in drinking water → V D. Growth of fish is stopped → caused by very low dissolved oxygen (1 ppm) in water → I Answer: (1) A-IV, B-II, C-V, D-I
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
A. Methemaglobinemia (Blue baby syndrome) → caused by excess nitrates in drinking water → IV

B. Kidney damage → caused by excess lead in drinking water → II

C. Bones and teeth damage → caused by excess fluoride in drinking water → V

D. Growth of fish is stopped → caused by very low dissolved oxygen (1 ppm) in water → I

Answer: (1) A-IV, B-II, C-V, D-I

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following statements is NOT correct for H2−O2 fuel cell?
questions.solutionText: Solution In an H 2 − O 2 fuel cell, the electrodes are typically porous carbon electrodes impregnated with catalysts like platinum, palladium, or nickel to facilitate the reactions. The use of platinum wires as electrodes is not standard practice because: Platinum is expensive, and using it as solid wires would not be cost-effective. Porous electrodes increase the surface area for the reaction and improve efficiency. Conclusion: This statement is NOT correct.

Porous electrodes increase the surface area for the reaction and improve efficiency. Conclusion: This statement is NOT correct.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
In an H2−O2 fuel cell, the electrodes are typically porous carbon electrodes impregnated with catalysts like platinum, palladium, or nickel to facilitate the reactions.
The use of platinum wires as electrodes is not standard practice because:
Platinum is expensive, and using it as solid wires would not be cost-effective.
Porous electrodes increase the surface area for the reaction and improve efficiency.
Conclusion: This statement is NOT correct.

questions.html: The test that is done for the differentiation of primary amines from secondary and tertiary amine is:
questions.questionHtml: The test that is done for the differentiation of primary amines from secondary and tertiary amine is:
questions.solutionText: Solution The Carbylamine reaction is a chemical test for detection of primary amines. In this reaction, the analyte is heated with alcoholic potassium hydroxide and chloroform. If a primary amine is present, the isocyanide is formed. For example, the reaction with ethylamine: C 2 H 5 NH 2 + CHCl 3 + 3 KOH → C 2 H 5 NC + 3 KCl + 3 H 2 O Ethylamine Chloroform Ethyl isocyanide
questions.solution: Solution The Carbylamine reaction is a chemical test for detection of primary amines. In this reaction, the analyte is heated with alcoholic potassium hydroxide and chloroform. questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

The Carbylamine reaction is a chemical test for detection of primary amines. In this reaction, the analyte is heated with alcoholic potassium hydroxide and chloroform. If a primary amine is present, the isocyanide is formed.

For example, the reaction with ethylamine:

C2H5NH2+CHCl3+3KOH→C2H5NC+3KCl+3H2OEthylamine Chloroform Ethyl isocyanide

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Paragraph: What carmakers often mean when they say a car is welldesigned, is that it appeals to men, particularly to their less noble instinct. 'beautiful body'. 'she must move like a dream'. But automacho is going out of style. In America, 47 % of new private cars are bought by women, up from 36 % in 1989 . Add the influence women have on a family's car-buying, and it is probable that women are more influential overall in choosing cars than men. So, carmakers are learning to create designs that appeal to them. Women tend to buy cheaper cars, largely because working women tend to have lower income. In America, they buy 55 % of the small cars, 44 % of medium sized ones, but only 28 % of large and luxurious models. They put more store on reliability than men doprobably a hidden reason for the rise in Japanese imports. American carmakers are now tailoring certain versions of their cars with women in mind. And they are learning that design can sway even normally pragmatic women as a comparison between Ford's Thunderbird and Mercury Cougar demonstrates. The Thunderbird is a high performance car - i.e., it goes fast and is styled to look aggressive. Less than 40 % of Thunderbirds are bought by women. But the same car with a more sedate body, a different name (The Cougar) and different advertising is as popular with women as it is with men. Question: Which of the following can be inferred from the passage?

questions.html:
Paragraph:
What carmakers often mean when they say a car is welldesigned, is that it appeals to men, particularly to their less noble instinct. 'beautiful body'. 'she must move like a dream'. But automacho is going out of style. In America, 47% of new private cars are bought by women, up from 36% in 1989 . Add the influence women have on a family's car-buying, and it is probable that women are more influential overall in choosing cars than men. So, carmakers are learning to create designs that appeal to them. Women tend to buy cheaper cars, largely because working women tend to have lower income. In America, they buy 55% of the small cars, 44% of medium sized ones, but only 28% of large and luxurious models. They put more store on reliability than men doprobably a hidden reason for the rise in Japanese imports.

American carmakers are now tailoring certain versions of their cars with women in mind. And they are learning that design can sway even normally pragmatic women as a comparison between Ford's Thunderbird and Mercury Cougar demonstrates. The Thunderbird is a high performance car - i.e., it goes fast and is styled to look aggressive. Less than 40% of Thunderbirds are bought by women. But the same car with a more sedate body, a different name (The Cougar) and different advertising is as popular with women as it is with men.

Question:
Which of the following can be inferred from the passage?
questions.questionHtml:
Paragraph:
What carmakers often mean when they say a car is welldesigned, is that it appeals to men, particularly to their less noble instinct. 'beautiful body'. 'she must move like a dream'. But automacho is going out of style. In America, 47% of new private cars are bought by women, up from 36% in 1989 . Add the influence women have on a family's car-buying, and it is probable that women are more influential overall in choosing cars than men. So, carmakers are learning to create designs that appeal to them. Women tend to buy cheaper cars, largely because working women tend to have lower income. In America, they buy 55% of the small cars, 44% of medium sized ones, but only 28% of large and luxurious models. They put more store on reliability than men doprobably a hidden reason for the rise in Japanese imports.

American carmakers are now tailoring certain versions of their cars with women in mind. And they are learning that design can sway even normally pragmatic women as a comparison between Ford's Thunderbird and Mercury Cougar demonstrates. The Thunderbird is a high performance car - i.e., it goes fast and is styled to look aggressive. Less than 40% of Thunderbirds are bought by women. But the same car with a more sedate body, a different name (The Cougar) and different advertising is as popular with women as it is with men.

Question:
Which of the following can be inferred from the passage?
questions.solutionText: Solution The passage compares Ford's Thunderbird (aggressive styling, less than 40% bought by women) with Mercury Cougar (same car, sedate body, different name and advertising, equally popular with women and men). This shows that when the same car is given a more sedate design and different advertising, it appeals more to women, thereby increasing its overall customer base. (1) Sedate cars with different advertising register more sales → This can be inferred from the Thunderbird vs Cougar comparison. The Cougar attracts both men and women equally, unlike the Thunderbird. (2) Small cars are gaining sales → The passage says women buy 55% of small cars, but does not say small car sales are increasing. (3) Medium cars are gaining sales → Not supported by the passage. (4) Luxurious cars are not selling at all → The passage says only 28% are bought by women, not that they aren't selling. Answer: (1) Sedate cars with different advertising register more sales
questions.solution: Solution The passage compares Ford's Thunderbird (aggressive styling, less than 40% bought by women) with Mercury Cougar (same car, sedate body, different name and advertising, equally popular with women and men). This shows that when the same car is given a more sedate design and different advertising, it appeals more to women, thereby increasing its overall customer base. (1) Sedate cars with different advertising register more sales → This can be inferred from the Thunderbird vs Cougar comparison. The Cougar attracts both men and women equally, unlike the Thunderbird. (2) Small cars are gaining sales → The passage says women buy 55% of small cars, but does not say small car sales are increasing. (3) Medium cars are gaining sales → Not supported by the passage. (4) Luxurious cars are not selling at all → The passage says only 28% are bought by women, not that they aren't selling. Answer: (1) Sedate cars with different advertising register more sales
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The passage compares Ford's Thunderbird (aggressive styling, less than 40% bought by women) with Mercury Cougar (same car, sedate body, different name and advertising, equally popular with women and men).

This shows that when the same car is given a more sedate design and different advertising, it appeals more to women, thereby increasing its overall customer base.

(1) Sedate cars with different advertising register more sales → This can be inferred from the Thunderbird vs Cougar comparison. The Cougar attracts both men and women equally, unlike the Thunderbird.

(2) Small cars are gaining sales → The passage says women buy 55% of small cars, but does not say small car sales are increasing.

(3) Medium cars are gaining sales → Not supported by the passage.

(4) Luxurious cars are not selling at all → The passage says only 28% are bought by women, not that they aren't selling.

Answer: (1) Sedate cars with different advertising register more sales

questions.solutionText: Solution CALUMNY means a false and malicious statement designed to damage someone's reputation (slander/defamation). questions.solutionText: Solution OBFUSCATE means to make something unclear, confusing, or difficult to understand. (1) Obliterate → to destroy completely (not a synonym) (2) Confound → to confuse or bewilder (synonym) (3) Pontificate → to speak in a pompous manner (not a synonym) (4) Illuminate → to make clear or bright (antonym) Answer: (2) Confound

A: The company also plans a foray into the service sector by setting up a chain of launderettes across the country.

B: Yet today, pre-cooked chapatis and ready-made mixes are a big market.

C: And that's not all.

D: Today the idea may appear a trifle ambitious but remember that it was not so long ago that the same things were said about the market for ready-to-eat foods and branded cereals.

S6: Disposable incomes are rising in the metros and big cities and time is at a premium.
questions.questionHtml:
Arrange the sentences A, B, C and D to form a logical sequence between sentences S1 and S6.

S1: This is a company that prides itself on its carefully matured extensive distribution blocks and mentor network.

A: The company also plans a foray into the service sector by setting up a chain of launderettes across the country.

B: Yet today, pre-cooked chapatis and ready-made mixes are a big market.

C: And that's not all.

D: Today the idea may appear a trifle ambitious but remember that it was not so long ago that the same things were said about the market for ready-to-eat foods and branded cereals.

S6: Disposable incomes are rising in the metros and big cities and time is at a premium.
questions.solutionText: Solution The correct order is: S1 → C → A → D → B → S6 C: "And that's not all." naturally follows S1 and signals an additional point. A then introduces that additional point: the company's plan to enter the service sector with launderettes. D refers to "the idea", which clearly points back to the launderette plan in A. B supports D by giving an example of how markets once doubted can become large. S6 explains why such markets are growing: rising disposable incomes and shortage of time.
questions.solution: Solution The correct order is: S1 → C → A → D → B → S6 C: "And that's not all." naturally follows S1 and signals an additional point. B supports D by giving an example of how markets once doubted can become large. S6 explains why such markets are growing: rising disposable incomes and shortage of time.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The correct order is: S1 → C → A → D → B → S6

C: "And that's not all." naturally follows S1 and signals an additional point.

A then introduces that additional point: the company's plan to enter the service sector with launderettes.

D refers to "the idea", which clearly points back to the launderette plan in A.

B supports D by giving an example of how markets once doubted can become large.

S6 explains why such markets are growing: rising disposable incomes and shortage of time.

questions.text: In the given sentence, a part is underlined. Four different ways of phrasing the underlined part are indicated. Choose the best alternative. Since we are living in Bombay for five years, we are reluctant to move to another city.

questions.questionHtml:
In the given sentence, a part is underlined. Four different ways of phrasing the underlined part are indicated. Choose the best alternative.

Since we are living in Bombay for five years, we are reluctant to move to another city.
questions.solutionText: Solution The sentence describes an action that started in the past and is still continuing ("for five years"). This requires the present perfect continuous tense. (1) Being that we living → grammatically incorrect (2) Since we were living → past continuous, implies action is over (3) Since we have been living → present perfect continuous, correct for an ongoing action from the past (4) Being that we have been living → "being that" is non-standard usage Answer: (3) Since we have been living
questions.solution: Solution The sentence describes an action that started in the past and is still continuing ("for five years"). This requires the present perfect continuous tense. (1) Being that we living → grammatically incorrect (2) Since we were living → past continuous, implies action is over (3) Since we have been living → present perfect continuous, correct for an ongoing action from the past (4) Being that we have been living → "being that" is non-standard usage Answer: (3) Since we have been living
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The sentence describes an action that started in the past and is still continuing ("for five years"). This requires the present perfect continuous tense.

(1) Being that we living → grammatically incorrect
(2) Since we were living → past continuous, implies action is over
(3) Since we have been living → present perfect continuous, correct for an ongoing action from the past
(4) Being that we have been living → "being that" is non-standard usage

Answer: (3) Since we have been living

questions.html: Which single word is the most precise formal synonym for the phrase "to paper over the cracks"?
questions.questionHtml: Which single word is the most precise formal synonym for the phrase "to paper over the cracks"?
questions.solutionText: Solution "To paper over the cracks" means to disguise or cover up problems superficially without actually solving them. (1) Eradicate → to completely destroy or remove (opposite intent) (2) Palliate → to make something less severe without removing the cause; to disguise the seriousness of (synonym) (3) Excoriate → to criticize harshly (unrelated) (4) Ameliorate → to make something better (implies actual improvement, not superficial cover-up) Answer: (2) Palliate
questions.solution: Solution "To paper over the cracks" means to disguise or cover up problems superficially without actually solving them. (1) Eradicate → to completely destroy or remove (opposite intent) (2) Palliate → to make something less severe without removing the cause; to disguise the seriousness of (synonym) (3) Excoriate → to criticize harshly (unrelated) (4) Ameliorate → to make something better (implies actual improvement, not superficial cover-up) Answer: (2) Palliate
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
"To paper over the cracks" means to disguise or cover up problems superficially without actually solving them.

(1) Eradicate → to completely destroy or remove (opposite intent)
(2) Palliate → to make something less severe without removing the cause; to disguise the seriousness of (synonym)
(3) Excoriate → to criticize harshly (unrelated)
(4) Ameliorate → to make something better (implies actual improvement, not superficial cover-up)

Answer: (2) Palliate

In the following question, some part of the sentence is underlined. Which of the options given below the sentence should replace the part underlined to make the sentence grammatically correct?

The subsidiary banks of the State bank of India were established by different special statues.
questions.solutionText: Solution The sentence says banks were "established by" something. Banks are established by laws/acts, not by status or states. "Statues" in the original is a spelling error. The correct word is "statutes" (meaning laws or acts passed by a legislative body). (1) special status → status means position/rank, not a law (2) special statutes → statutes means formal laws/acts (correct) (3) especial states → grammatically awkward and wrong meaning (4) specialised statutes → "specialised" is redundant and unnatural here Answer: (2) special statutes
questions.solution: Solution The sentence says banks were "established by" something. Banks are established by laws/acts, not by status or states. "Statues" in the original is a spelling error. The correct word is "statutes" (meaning laws or acts passed by a legislative body). (1) special status → status means position/rank, not a law (2) special statutes → statutes means formal laws/acts (correct) (3) especial states → grammatically awkward and wrong meaning (4) specialised statutes → "specialised" is redundant and unnatural here Answer: (2) special statutes
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The sentence says banks were "established by" something. Banks are established by laws/acts, not by status or states.

"Statues" in the original is a spelling error. The correct word is "statutes" (meaning laws or acts passed by a legislative body).

(1) special status → status means position/rank, not a law
(2) special statutes → statutes means formal laws/acts (correct)
(3) especial states → grammatically awkward and wrong meaning
(4) specialised statutes → "specialised" is redundant and unnatural here

Answer: (2) special statutes

questions.solutionText: Solution A: The cultural programme consisted of different tastes. B: _______________. C: After the prayer, there was a dance item. Sentence C mentions "after the prayer", so sentence B must introduce a prayer. An invocation is a prayer or request for help at the beginning of an event. (1) It began with an invocation → introduces the prayer, logically followed by C ("after the prayer..."). (2) Finally the National Anthem was sung → this would be at the end, not the beginning. (3) When the skit ended there was a group dance → no connection to prayer. (4) The dance was followed by a skit → no connection to prayer. Answer: (1) It began with an invocation.
questions.solution: Solution A: The cultural programme consisted of different tastes. B: _______________. C: After the prayer, there was a dance item. Sentence C mentions "after the prayer", so sentence B must introduce a prayer. An invocation is a prayer or request for help at the beginning of an event. (1) It began with an invocation → introduces the prayer, logically followed by C ("after the prayer..."). (2) Finally the National Anthem was sung → this would be at the end, not the beginning. (3) When the skit ended there was a group dance → no connection to prayer. (4) The dance was followed by a skit → no connection to prayer. Answer: (1) It began with an invocation.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
A: The cultural programme consisted of different tastes.
B: _______________.
C: After the prayer, there was a dance item.

Sentence C mentions "after the prayer", so sentence B must introduce a prayer. An invocation is a prayer or request for help at the beginning of an event.

(1) It began with an invocation → introduces the prayer, logically followed by C ("after the prayer...").
(2) Finally the National Anthem was sung → this would be at the end, not the beginning.
(3) When the skit ended there was a group dance → no connection to prayer.
(4) The dance was followed by a skit → no connection to prayer.

Answer: (1) It began with an invocation.

questions.questionHtml: Pariniti ranked 17th from the top and 28th from the bottom among those who made it to the final round of Miss India Contest. 7 girls did not participate in the competition and 7 failed in the previous round. How many girls enrolled in the contest?
questions.solutionText: Solution Girls in the final round = 17 + 28 − 1 = 44 7 did not participate + 7 failed in previous round = 14 Total girls enrolled = 44 + 14 = 58 Answer: 58
questions.solution: Solution Girls in the final round = 17 + 28 − 1 = 44 7 did not participate + 7 failed in previous round = 14 Total girls enrolled = 44 + 14 = 58 Answer: 58
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Girls in the final round =17+28−1=44

7 did not participate + 7 failed in previous round =14

Total girls enrolled =44+14=58

Answer: 58

questions.questionHtml:
Statement: It is a paradox at one level that we talk of the complete elimination of poverty while many of the pallbearers of our culture have romanticized poverty.

From the above one can conclude:
questions.solutionText: Solution The statement highlights a paradox: we aim to eliminate poverty, yet cultural figures (pallbearers of our culture) have romanticized it, implying poverty has been deeply embedded in society. (1) Poverty can never be eliminated → too absolute, not implied (2) Poverty should not be eliminated → not the message (3) Complete elimination of poverty will not be allowed → not implied (4) Poverty has been an inescapable part of social milieu, and it is difficult to eliminate it → correctly captures the paradox: poverty is deeply rooted in culture, making elimination difficult Answer: (4) Poverty has been an inescapable part of social milieu, and it is difficult to eliminate it
questions.solution: Solution The statement highlights a paradox: we aim to eliminate poverty, yet cultural figures (pallbearers of our culture) have romanticized it, implying poverty has been deeply embedded in society. (1) Poverty can never be eliminated → too absolute, not implied (2) Poverty should not be eliminated → not the message (3) Complete elimination of poverty will not be allowed → not implied (4) Poverty has been an inescapable part of social milieu, and it is difficult to eliminate it → correctly captures the paradox: poverty is deeply rooted in culture, making elimination difficult Answer: (4) Poverty has been an inescapable part of social milieu, and it is difficult to eliminate it
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The statement highlights a paradox: we aim to eliminate poverty, yet cultural figures (pallbearers of our culture) have romanticized it, implying poverty has been deeply embedded in society.

(1) Poverty can never be eliminated → too absolute, not implied
(2) Poverty should not be eliminated → not the message
(3) Complete elimination of poverty will not be allowed → not implied
(4) Poverty has been an inescapable part of social milieu, and it is difficult to eliminate it → correctly captures the paradox: poverty is deeply rooted in culture, making elimination difficult

Answer: (4) Poverty has been an inescapable part of social milieu, and it is difficult to eliminate it

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: In a certain code, BELIEF is written as AFKKDH. How would SELDOM be written in that code?
questions.questionText: In a certain code, BELIEF is written as AFKKDH. How would SELDOM be written in that code?
questions.question: In a certain code, BELIEF is written as AFKKDH. How would SELDOM be written in that code?
questions.prompt: In a certain code, BELIEF is written as AFKKDH. How would SELDOM be written in that code?
questions.html: In a certain code, BELIEF is written as AFKKDH. How would SELDOM be written in that code?
questions.questionHtml: In a certain code, BELIEF is written as AFKKDH. How would SELDOM be written in that code?
questions.solutionText: Solution Given: BELIEF is Coded as AFKKDH. We can easily get that, BELIEF is coded by subtracting ' 1 ' from its odd positioned letter and by adding ' 1 , 2 , 2 ' to the corresponding even positioned letters. i.e., B ⟶ - 1 A E ⟶ + 1 F L ⟶ - 1 K I ⟶ + 2 K E ⟶ - 1 D F ⟶ + 2 H Similarly, S ⟶ - 1 R E ⟶ + 1 F L ⟶ - 1 K D ⟶ + 2 F O ⟶ - 1 N M ⟶ + 2 O Hence, SELDOM is coded as RFKFNO.
questions.solution: Solution Given: BELIEF is Coded as AFKKDH. We can easily get that, BELIEF is coded by subtracting ' 1 ' from its odd positioned letter and by adding ' 1 , 2 , 2 ' to the corresponding even positioned letters. i.e., B ⟶ - 1 A E ⟶ + 1 F L ⟶ - 1 K I ⟶ + 2 K E ⟶ - 1 D F ⟶ + 2 H Similarly, S ⟶ - 1 R E ⟶ + 1 F L ⟶ - 1 K D ⟶ + 2 F O ⟶ - 1 N M ⟶ + 2 O Hence, SELDOM is coded as RFKFNO.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Given:

BELIEF is Coded as AFKKDH.
We can easily get that,

BELIEF is coded by subtracting '1' from its odd positioned letter and by adding '1, 2, 2' to the corresponding even positioned letters.

i.e.,

B ⟶-1 A

E ⟶+1 F

L ⟶-1 K

I ⟶+2 K

E ⟶-1 D

F ⟶+2 H

Similarly,

S ⟶-1 R

E ⟶+1 F

L ⟶-1 K

D ⟶+2 F

O ⟶-1 N

M ⟶+2 O

Hence, SELDOM is coded as RFKFNO.

questions.questionHtml:
In the question below there are given some statements followed by some conclusions. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts. Read all the conclusions and then decide which of the given conclusions logically follows/follow from the given statements, disregarding commonly known facts.

Statements:
A few parrots are not pigeons.
All pigeons are white.
No white is green.

Conclusions:
I. No green is a pigeon.
II. No parrot is green.
III. Some pigeons are not parrots.
questions.solutionText: Solution Statements: 1. A few parrots are not pigeons. (Some parrots are not pigeons) 2. All pigeons are white. 3. No white is green. From (2) and (3): All pigeons are white + No white is green ⇒ No pigeon is green. I. No green is a pigeon → This is the converse of "No pigeon is green", which is logically equivalent. (Follows) II. No parrot is green → We only know "a few parrots are not pigeons", meaning some parrots may or may not be pigeons. Parrots that are not pigeons and not white could be green. We cannot definitively say no parrot is green. (Does not necessarily follow) III. Some pigeons are not parrots → From "a few parrots are not pigeons", we cannot conclude anything definite about pigeons not being parrots. It is possible that all pigeons are parrots. (Does not necessarily follow) Answer: (1) Only C1 follows
questions.solution: Solution Statements: 1. A few parrots are not pigeons. (Some parrots are not pigeons) 2. All pigeons are white. 3. No white is green. From (2) and (3): All pigeons are white + No white is green ⇒ No pigeon is green. I. No green is a pigeon → This is the converse of "No pigeon is green", which is logically equivalent. (Follows) II. No parrot is green → We only know "a few parrots are not pigeons", meaning some parrots may or may not be pigeons. Parrots that are not pigeons and not white could be green. We cannot definitively say no parrot is green. (Does not necessarily follow) III. Some pigeons are not parrots → From "a few parrots are not pigeons", we cannot conclude anything definite about pigeons not being parrots. It is possible that all pigeons are parrots. (Does not necessarily follow) Answer: (1) Only C1 follows
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Statements:
1. A few parrots are not pigeons. (Some parrots are not pigeons)
2. All pigeons are white.
3. No white is green.

From (2) and (3): All pigeons are white + No white is green ⇒ No pigeon is green.

I. No green is a pigeon → This is the converse of "No pigeon is green", which is logically equivalent. (Follows)

II. No parrot is green → We only know "a few parrots are not pigeons", meaning some parrots may or may not be pigeons. Parrots that are not pigeons and not white could be green. We cannot definitively say no parrot is green. (Does not necessarily follow)

III. Some pigeons are not parrots → From "a few parrots are not pigeons", we cannot conclude anything definite about pigeons not being parrots. It is possible that all pigeons are parrots. (Does not necessarily follow)

Answer: (1) Only C1 follows

questions.text: Shrikant is shorter than Nilima. Pratima is taller than Shrikant. Subhash is taller than Nilima but shorter than Heramb. Nilima is taller than Pratima. Who will be in the middle if they stand in a row according to the descending order of height?
questions.questionText: Shrikant is shorter than Nilima. Pratima is taller than Shrikant. Subhash is taller than Nilima but shorter than Heramb. Nilima is taller than Pratima. Who will be in the middle if they stand in a row according to the descending order of height?

questions.questionHtml: Shrikant is shorter than Nilima. Pratima is taller than Shrikant. Subhash is taller than Nilima but shorter than Heramb. Nilima is taller than Pratima. Who will be in the middle if they stand in a row according to the descending order of height?
questions.solutionText: Solution Shrikant < Nilima Pratima > Shrikant Nilima < Subhash < Heramb Nilima > Pratima Combining: Shrikant < Pratima < Nilima < Subhash < Heramb Descending order: Heramb > Subhash > Nilima > Pratima > Shrikant Middle (3rd position) = Nilima Answer: (4) Nilima
questions.solution: Solution Shrikant < Nilima Pratima > Shrikant Nilima < Subhash < Heramb Nilima > Pratima Combining: Shrikant < Pratima < Nilima < Subhash < Heramb Descending order: Heramb > Subhash > Nilima > Pratima > Shrikant Middle (3rd position) = Nilima Answer: (4) Nilima
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Shrikant < Nilima
Pratima > Shrikant
Nilima < Subhash < Heramb
Nilima > Pratima

Combining: Shrikant < Pratima < Nilima < Subhash < Heramb

Descending order: Heramb > Subhash > Nilima > Pratima > Shrikant

Middle (3rd position) = Nilima

Answer: (4) Nilima

questions.questionHtml:
In a question below are given three statements followed by four conclusions numbered I, II, III and IV. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts. Read all the conclusions and then decide which of the given conclusions logically follows from the given statements, disregarding commonly known facts.

Statements:
Some stones are not rods.
All rods are black.
No black is a crow.

Conclusions:
I Some crows are rods.
II. Some blacks are stones.
III. Some crows are blacks.
IV. Some stones are rods.
questions.solutionText: Solution Statements: 1. Some stones are not rods. 2. All rods are black. 3. No black is a crow. From (2) and (3): All rods are black + No black is a crow ⇒ No rod is a crow ⇒ No crow is a rod. I. Some crows are rods → No crow is a rod (from above), so this is false. (Does not follow) II. Some blacks are stones → We only know some stones are not rods. Those stones that are rods would be black (from statement 2), but we don't know if any stones are rods for certain. "Some stones are not rods" does not guarantee some stones are rods. (Does not necessarily follow) III. Some crows are blacks → No black is a crow means no crow is black. (Does not follow) IV. Some stones are rods → "Some stones are not rods" only tells us at least some stones are not rods. It does not guarantee that some stones are rods. (Does not necessarily follow) Answer: (2) None follows
questions.solution: Solution Statements: 1. Some stones are not rods. 2. All rods are black. 3. No black is a crow. From (2) and (3): All rods are black + No black is a crow ⇒ No rod is a crow ⇒ No crow is a rod. I. Some crows are rods → No crow is a rod (from above), so this is false. (Does not follow) II. Some blacks are stones → We only know some stones are not rods. Those stones that are rods would be black (from statement 2), but we don't know if any stones are rods for certain. "Some stones are not rods" does not guarantee some stones are rods. (Does not necessarily follow) III. Some crows are blacks → No black is a crow means no crow is black. (Does not follow) IV. Some stones are rods → "Some stones are not rods" only tells us at least some stones are not rods. It does not guarantee that some stones are rods. (Does not necessarily follow) Answer: (2) None follows
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Statements:
1. Some stones are not rods.
2. All rods are black.
3. No black is a crow.

From (2) and (3): All rods are black + No black is a crow ⇒ No rod is a crow ⇒ No crow is a rod.

I. Some crows are rods → No crow is a rod (from above), so this is false. (Does not follow)

II. Some blacks are stones → We only know some stones are not rods. Those stones that are rods would be black (from statement 2), but we don't know if any stones are rods for certain. "Some stones are not rods" does not guarantee some stones are rods. (Does not necessarily follow)

III. Some crows are blacks → No black is a crow means no crow is black. (Does not follow)

IV. Some stones are rods → "Some stones are not rods" only tells us at least some stones are not rods. It does not guarantee that some stones are rods. (Does not necessarily follow)

Answer: (2) None follows

questions.solutionText: Solution As per the question, If the figure is folded to make a cube, then H and I share's an edge and the one end of this edge is the face labelled G . So the faces G , H , I meet at a point, and at the other end of this edge is the face labelled J . i.e., G is opposite to J . H is opposite to K . I is opposite to S . Hence, the answer is J .

Solution

As per the question,

If the figure is folded to make a cube, then H and I share's an edge and the one end of this edge is the face labelled G. So the faces G, H, I meet at a point, and at the other end of this edge is the face labelled J.

i.e., G is opposite to J.

H is opposite to K.

I is opposite to S.

Hence, the answer is J.

questions.html:
What could be the code for "and this is how" according to the given code language?

Given Codes:
this is not me → ar rt st pt
not you but him → mn we rt qs
me and you come → yu mn pt xr
but this is him → ar we st qs
and play → yu lk
questions.questionHtml:
What could be the code for "and this is how" according to the given code language?

Given Codes:
this is not me → ar rt st pt
not you but him → mn we rt qs
me and you come → yu mn pt xr
but this is him → ar we st qs
and play → yu lk
questions.solutionText: Solution Comparing the first and fourth statements: "this is not me" → ar rt st pt "but this is him" → ar we st qs Common words: "this" and "is" → Common codes: ar and st So, this/is = ar/st Comparing the third and fifth statements: "me and you come" → yu mn pt xr "and play" → yu lk Common word: "and" → Common code: yu So, and = yu Now we need the code for "and this is how". From our deductions, the code must contain: yu (and), ar (this/is), st (this/is). The word "how" is not present in any given sentence, so its code must be a new one. Checking options: (1) ar st yu pm → contains ar, st, yu and pm (new code for "how") → Valid (2) ar st xr we → does not contain yu (code for "and") → Invalid (3) ar mn yu st → contains mn which is the code for "you" (not needed) → Invalid (4) st pt xr yu → does not contain ar → Invalid Answer: (1) ar st yu pm
questions.solution: Solution Comparing the first and fourth statements: "this is not me" → ar rt st pt "but this is him" → ar we st qs Common words: "this" and "is" → Common codes: ar and st So, this/is = ar/st Comparing the third and fifth statements: "me and you come" → yu mn pt xr "and play" → yu lk Common word: "and" → Common code: yu So, and = yu Now we need the code for "and this is how". From our deductions, the code must contain: yu (and), ar (this/is), st (this/is). The word "how" is not present in any given sentence, so its code must be a new one. Checking options: (1) ar st yu pm → contains ar, st, yu and pm (new code for "how") → Valid (2) ar st xr we → does not contain yu (code for "and") → Invalid (3) ar mn yu st → contains mn which is the code for "you" (not needed) → Invalid (4) st pt xr yu → does not contain ar → Invalid Answer: (1) ar st yu pm
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Comparing the first and fourth statements:
"this is not me" → ar rt st pt
"but this is him" → ar we st qs
Common words: "this" and "is" → Common codes: ar and st
So, this/is = ar/st

Comparing the third and fifth statements:
"me and you come" → yu mn pt xr
"and play" → yu lk
Common word: "and" → Common code: yu
So, and = yu

Now we need the code for "and this is how".
From our deductions, the code must contain: yu (and), ar (this/is), st (this/is).
The word "how" is not present in any given sentence, so its code must be a new one.

Checking options:
(1) ar st yu pm → contains ar, st, yu and pm (new code for "how") → Valid
(2) ar st xr we → does not contain yu (code for "and") → Invalid
(3) ar mn yu st → contains mn which is the code for "you" (not needed) → Invalid
(4) st pt xr yu → does not contain ar → Invalid

Answer: (1) ar st yu pm

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Determine whether the positive integer x is a multiple of 4. (1) The unit digit of x is 4. (2) The sum of x and 6 is exactly divisible by 8.
questions.questionText: Determine whether the positive integer x is a multiple of 4. (1) The unit digit of x is 4. (2) The sum of x and 6 is exactly divisible by 8.
questions.question: Determine whether the positive integer x is a multiple of 4. (1) The unit digit of x is 4. (2) The sum of x and 6 is exactly divisible by 8.
questions.prompt: Determine whether the positive integer x is a multiple of 4. (1) The unit digit of x is 4. (2) The sum of x and 6 is exactly divisible by 8.
questions.html:
Determine whether the positive integer x is a multiple of 4.

(1) The unit digit of x is 4.
(2) The sum of x and 6 is exactly divisible by 8.
questions.questionHtml:
Determine whether the positive integer x is a multiple of 4.

(1) The unit digit of x is 4.
(2) The sum of x and 6 is exactly divisible by 8.
questions.solutionText: Solution Goal: Determine if x is divisible by 4. (We need a definitive YES or NO). Evaluating Statement (1): The unit digit of x is 4. If x = 4 , it is divisible by 4 (Yes). If x = 14 , it is not divisible by 4 (No). Since we get both Yes and No, this statement is insufficient. Evaluating Statement (2): The sum of x and 6 is exactly divisible by 8. x + 6 = 8 k (where k is an integer) x = 8 k − 6 = 8 k − 4 − 2 = 4 ( 2 k − 1 ) − 2 Since 4 ( 2 k − 1 ) is a multiple of 4, subtracting 2 means x is exactly 2 units away from a multiple of 4. Example: If x + 6 = 16 , then x = 10 (not divisible by 4). Since x will never be divisible by 4, we have a definitive NO. This statement is sufficient. Answer: (2) Statement (2) ALONE is sufficient to answer the question, but statement (1) alone is not.
questions.solution: Solution Goal: Determine if x is divisible by 4. (We need a definitive YES or NO). Evaluating Statement (1): The unit digit of x is 4. If x = 4 , it is divisible by 4 (Yes). If x = 14 , it is not divisible by 4 (No). Since we get both Yes and No, this statement is insufficient. Evaluating Statement (2): The sum of x and 6 is exactly divisible by 8. x + 6 = 8 k (where k is an integer) x = 8 k − 6 = 8 k − 4 − 2 = 4 ( 2 k − 1 ) − 2 Since 4 ( 2 k − 1 ) is a multiple of 4, subtracting 2 means x is exactly 2 units away from a multiple of 4. Example: If x + 6 = 16 , then x = 10 (not divisible by 4). Since x will never be divisible by 4, we have a definitive NO. This statement is sufficient. Answer: (2) Statement (2) ALONE is sufficient to answer the question, but statement (1) alone is not.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Goal: Determine if x is divisible by 4. (We need a definitive YES or NO).

Evaluating Statement (1): The unit digit of x is 4.
If x=4, it is divisible by 4 (Yes).
If x=14, it is not divisible by 4 (No).
Since we get both Yes and No, this statement is insufficient.

Evaluating Statement (2): The sum of x and 6 is exactly divisible by 8.
x+6=8k (where k is an integer)
x=8k−6=8k−4−2=4(2k−1)−2

Since 4(2k−1) is a multiple of 4, subtracting 2 means x is exactly 2 units away from a multiple of 4.
Example: If x+6=16, then x=10 (not divisible by 4).
Since x will never be divisible by 4, we have a definitive NO. This statement is sufficient.

Answer: (2) Statement (2) ALONE is sufficient to answer the question, but statement (1) alone is not.

questions.questionHtml:
Which of the following are correct ways of arguing?

(i) There can be no second husband without a second wife.

(ii) Anil is a friend of Bob, Bob is a friend of Raj, and hence Anil is a friend of Raj.

(iii) A is equal to B, B is equal to C, hence A is equal to C.

questions.solutionText: Solution (i) There can be no second husband without a second wife → This is a logically valid argument based on a definitional relationship. (Correct) (ii) Anil is a friend of Bob, Bob is a friend of Raj, hence Anil is a friend of Raj → Friendship is not transitive. A's friend's friend need not be A's friend. (Incorrect) (iii) A = B, B = C, hence A = C → Equality is transitive. This is a valid logical argument. (Correct) (iv) If everyone is a liar, then we cannot prove it → This is a valid paradox-based argument. If everyone is a liar, any claim (including this one) would be a lie, making it impossible to prove. (Correct) Answer: (i), (iii) and (iv) are correct ways of arguing.
questions.solution: Solution (i) There can be no second husband without a second wife → This is a logically valid argument based on a definitional relationship. (Correct) (ii) Anil is a friend of Bob, Bob is a friend of Raj, hence Anil is a friend of Raj → Friendship is not transitive. A's friend's friend need not be A's friend. (Incorrect) (iii) A = B, B = C, hence A = C → Equality is transitive. This is a valid logical argument. (Correct) (iv) If everyone is a liar, then we cannot prove it → This is a valid paradox-based argument. If everyone is a liar, any claim (including this one) would be a lie, making it impossible to prove. (Correct) Answer: (i), (iii) and (iv) are correct ways of arguing.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
(i) There can be no second husband without a second wife → This is a logically valid argument based on a definitional relationship. (Correct)

(ii) Anil is a friend of Bob, Bob is a friend of Raj, hence Anil is a friend of Raj → Friendship is not transitive. A's friend's friend need not be A's friend. (Incorrect)

(iii) A = B, B = C, hence A = C → Equality is transitive. This is a valid logical argument. (Correct)

(iv) If everyone is a liar, then we cannot prove it → This is a valid paradox-based argument. If everyone is a liar, any claim (including this one) would be a lie, making it impossible to prove. (Correct)

Answer: (i), (iii) and (iv) are correct ways of arguing.

questions.solutionText: Solution ACFJ → OUZJ Pattern: A → O (+14), C → U (+18), F → Z (+20), J → J (+26 i.e. same letter) Differences: +14, +18, +20, +26 Applying same shifts to SUXB: S + 14 = G U + 18 = M X + 20 = R B + 26 = B Answer: (4) GMRB
questions.solution: Solution ACFJ → OUZJ Pattern: A → O (+14), C → U (+18), F → Z (+20), J → J (+26 i.e. same letter) Differences: +14, +18, +20, +26 Applying same shifts to SUXB: S + 14 = G U + 18 = M X + 20 = R B + 26 = B Answer: (4) GMRB
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
ACFJ → OUZJ

Pattern: A → O (+14), C → U (+18), F → Z (+20), J → J (+26 i.e. same letter)

Differences: +14, +18, +20, +26

Applying same shifts to SUXB:
S + 14 = G
U + 18 = M
X + 20 = R
B + 26 = B

Answer: (4) GMRB

questions.solutionText: Solution Since, 1 a , 1 b and 1 c are in A P . ∴ 1 a + 1 c = 2 b . . . i Now, 1 a + 1 b - 1 c 1 b + 1 c - 1 a = 1 a + 1 b - 2 b - 1 a 1 b + 1 c - 2 b - 1 c [from equation i ] = 2 a - 1 b 2 c - 1 b = 4 a c - 2 b 1 a + 1 c + 1 b 2 = 4 a c - 2 b 2 b + 1 b 2 = 4 a c - 3 b 2

[from equation i]

questions.questionHtml: If the distance between the foci of an ellipse be equal to its minor axis, then its eccentricity is
questions.solutionText: Solution Distance between the foci is 2ae Length of the minor axis = 2 b ∴ 2 ae = 2 b ⇒ ae = b ⇒ b 2 = a 2 ( 1 − e 2 ) ⇒ a 2 e 2 = a 2 − a 2 e 2 ⇒ 2 a 2 e 2 = a 2 ⇒ e 2 = 1 2 ⇒ e = 1 2 Hence, eccentricity of the ellipse = 1 2
questions.solution: Solution Distance between the foci is 2ae Length of the minor axis = 2 b ∴ 2 ae = 2 b ⇒ ae = b ⇒ b 2 = a 2 ( 1 − e 2 ) ⇒ a 2 e 2 = a 2 − a 2 e 2 ⇒ 2 a 2 e 2 = a 2 ⇒ e 2 = 1 2 ⇒ e = 1 2 Hence, eccentricity of the ellipse = 1 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Distance between the foci is 2ae
Length of the minor axis =2b

Hence, eccentricity of the ellipse =12
